+// Group returns the API group part of metadata.name.

body:
No offense, but this is a useless comment :)

Scope only has 2 valid values, right?  ThisContext and ParentContext.  So this whole section is effectively "if the scope is not ThisContext, set it to ThisContext".  The conditional is noise?  It could be replaced with `scope = ThisContext`.  But that's not my main point.

What I want to understand is WHY this is correct.

If I found a Deferred validation which specifically asks for ParentContext, why is it OK to just convert that to ThisContext?  On the surface that seems wrong, so please analyze and explain why it is correct and safe -- how could one end up in this situation?  

Here's how I understand it, please tell me if I am wrong.

Consider a case like:

```
// +k8s:item(key: "x")=+k8s:childTag1
// +k8s:item(key: "x")=+k8s:childTag2
```

Assume childTag1 produces a Function("abc") and a Deferred("def"), while childTag2 produces a Function("xyz").

There are two ways to interpret that:

Simple: We don't accumulate item calls per criteria, so "deferred" really has no meaning here.  Handling the first tag-line, we would call "abc" followed by "def", and then we would do the second tag-line and call "xyz".

Correct: We accumulate item calls per criteria, so "deferred" means "after all non-deferred tags for this criteria".  We would have to collect "abc" and "xyz", keyed by the criteria, and then do "def" after both of those.

IMO, the "correct" approach is correct, but I am not convinced it will actually matter, and we probably should not waste time on it.

Assuming my assessment is close to correct - this is the sort of comment I want - the full analysis.  This way, some future you or me who realizes we actually do need to accumulate will remember WHY we did it this way -- not because it was Correct but because it was Good Enough.

title: feat: flowcontrol LimitResponse and PriorityLevelConfigurationSpec discriminator and member dv migration
body:
I agree the implicit forbidden is not obvious.  The implication then is that EVERY field which is a member of a mode-set should specify its behavior in EVERY value for the discriminator.

If we could enforce that, I would get behind it eagerly!  The problem is when you add a new possible value to the discriminator, you need to go touch all the other fields, which is (IMO) acceptable.  Having it be inconsistent seems the worst choice..

OTOH - that sort of requires that discriminators should be enum-strings (it looks like we currently support and test bool and int discriminators -- can we kill those before they attract use?)

# shellcheck disable=2046 # printf word-splitting is intentional

We should not have fields that claim to be both `+optional` and `+k8s:required`.  That indicates something went wrong.

title: KEP-5732: Add SchedulingConstraints to PodGroup API and use them in TopologyPlacement plugin
body:
> a) forbid topologyConstraints = [] (non-nil length=0)

We can't really tell the difference between `nil` and `[]` when it gets to the wire.  Since you are calling this "optional", both of these should be detected as "not specified" (because `len() == 0`).

> b) forbid schedulingConstraints = {}

That's what `unionMember` does.  

Let's back up -- do you think `TopologyConstraints` will always need at least one item, or will it eventually be allowed to be empty because some OTHER mode of specifying constraints will be defined?

Be very cautious about combinations.  If you say something like "all of the fields will be ANDed", then it's simple enough, and `{}` is logically consistent (if a bit ugly).  But if you want to say something like "Field B modifes the meaning of A" or "field C is mutually exclusive with A but combines with B", then you end up with a mess -- hard to implement correctly, hard to understand, hard to document.  See `Service` for the canonical counter-example.

title: [Declarative Validation] Add `k8s:minLength` tag
body:
> I think we should try to only have one semantically correct way to represent something, and like Jordan pointed out, setting +k8s:minLength=0 is the same as just not validating for a minimum length so it doesn't do anything but add unnecessary generated validation logic.

I don't feel strongly, but I will argue anyway -- it expresses an explicit intention, as opposed to incidental behavior.

If I see a field with no minLength, does that mean it is supposed to allows 0, forgot to check, or is covered by something else (e.g. `+k8s:format`).  Even still, not every format is clearly checking minimums.

Should we demand that every string has a format tag and that every format document the minimum?  If so, we don't really need minLength or maxLength, those will just cause 2 errors for the same problem.

Should we demasnd that every string has minLength and maxLength?  Formats might double up on this and cause 2 errors for the same problem.

I think what I am saying is that we should let the API authors/reviewers decide whether and when to use which tags, and that minLength=0 *is* semantically meaningful.  More than that, disallowing it "because it doesn't mean anything" is worse than just having it mean nothing (like literally - we could choose to NOT emit a validation when the tag value is exaclty 0).

Ahh, right.  Yeah it does not work on the thing that Go calls an "alias" because Go's tooling doesn't give us enough information to do that.  That's intentional on their part - it is an alias, not a new type.  It should be behaviorally 100% identical in all regards.  `type Foo = Bar`

The thing that Gengo calls an "Alias" is what C would call a "typedef" and Go doesn't really have a distinct name for: `type Foo Bar`.  That should work in DV.

RE test fail:  You marked all these fields as optional, which (for value-typed fields) means "not the zero value".  If we find the zero value we skip subsequent validation -- how can something be both "not specified" and "invalid value" at the same time?  :)

title: Create Workload API v1alpha2
body:
A few things here:

1) add a comment to the effect of "this is a one-of set, but there's only one option present at the moment"

2) Don't treat a pointer to "" as equivalent to nil.  If the field is nil you can can return an error (`Invalid()` is correct here, as it aligns with DV: staging/src/k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/api/validate/union.go.  If they specify `""` then they should get an Invalid error about the name format.

title: Create Workload API v1alpha2
body:
the field name is already in the error (as a field.Path), so the detail string shoudl not repeat the name.  Otherwise you get something like:  "Invalid: path.to.podGroupName: podGroupName must not be empty".  Just "must not be empty" is OK.  In DV it would be tagged as a union, so you would get something like "must specify one of: podGroupName" on the parent, but we can adjust that when we are ready to use DV in pods.

@lalitc375 @aaron-prindle @yongruilin

title: Create Workload API v1alpha2
body:
This is a situation I don't think I have seen before -- we want to EOL a v1alpha1 type but not the whole apigroup.

@liggitt for consult.

Getting rid of a whole alpha apigroup is considered acceptable because it was alpha anyway, so we're OK with any detritus left behind.  But getting rid of a type while keeping the apigroup...I don't know if we have done that before.  There's a kind of assumption that if a "lower" apigroup exists, a type in a "higher" group can be accessed through the lower API, which would fail if it doesn't exist.

But actually, is that true?  If we were to add a new type to "core", it would need to go thru v1alpha1 and we probably would not re-populate pod and service and replicationcontroller and ... in v1a1, right?  This is a corner of the apigroup versioning that has bothered me for a very long time.

body:
Would you put `DesiredCount` inside the `PodGroup.Spec.SchedulingPolicy.Gang`?  Or would you define a parallel `PodGroup.Spec.SchedulingConfig.Gang` ?

```
PodGroup:
    metadata:
        name: my-pod-group
    spec:
        schedulingPolicy:
            gang:
                minCount: 5
                desiredCount: 10
```

vs.

```
PodGroup:
    metadata:
        name: my-pod-group
    spec:
        schedulingPolicy:
            gang:
                minCount: 5
        schedulingConfig:
            gang:
                desiredCount: 10
```

The former means you either a) put `desiredCount` in and just tell people "don't configure that in the template" (or at least define the behavior when they do) or b) you deep-copy the schema and commit to not colliding.

The latter means you deep-copy the skeleton of the schema (`basic`, `gang`, ...) and impose the UX on users.

body:
There is an age-old debate about "kind" vs "resource".  Kind is ambiguous -- a group CAN have multiple resources with the same Kind.  It's not common, but it's allowed.  Usually when we have a ref to an object of unknown bounds (i.e. the thing which reads this ref does not know a priori what a Kind means), then we switcht to Resource.  It's somewhat less pleasant UX and it's an ugly inconsistency (sometimes kind, sometimes resource), but that's the rule we have laid out.

IOW:

```
    kind: Deployment
```

becomes

```
    resource: deployments
```

Am I right in reading this as an open-ended reference?  I will open a discussion on api-reviews slack, in case sentiment has swung, but last I looked....

body:
Let's talk about the `alpha` designation here.

What `alpha` means in DV is that the handwritten validation is authoritative, and DV is checked against that to find any inconsistencies.  This being a new API, I don't WANT handwritten code.  I don't want to think about it and I don't want to review it :)

The DV gate is locked on and this is an alpha API group. I think we can say "DV is the only validation for this group" (which is the goal of DV!!).  If we set `WithDeclarativeEnforcement()` in strategy, we can remove most or all of the handwritten validation.

@yongruilin

no, I mean cases where you have two (top-level) resources which use the same Kind (type / schema).  We don't do it in k/k but some downstream things do (or did).  And yes, OwnerRefs do use Kind, so that's a good argument for why this rule should be considered obsolete.

https://kubernetes.slack.com/team/U0AH4GABW

> I'm not sure what open-ended reference means in this context, however - could you elaborate?

Example:  Gateway API allows refereces to arbitrary types,  but the controller for a given Gateway (as per the class) either knows what the {apigroup, kind} means or else it is an error.  That's NOT open-ended; there is a fixed set of possible types that can be referenced, but the set depends on the controller.

Here it could be ANY type in ANY group, and there's no way to know what it might be.

Don't act on this yet - let's see if we can make an argument to kill this rule.

body:
@yongruilin -- I think that resources which are new (alpha), which are not doing new handwritten code, do we still need to do this "it's not stable" dance?  

On one hand, the whole apigroup is alpha, so I don't care that the tag is not stable.  I don't want to read handwritten code.

On the other hand, we can't let the API go to GA while the tag is still alpha.

Is this the best we can do?  It's not terrible since it's 1:1 with a handwritten function, but play it out in the future.

A new API is being built.  It needs a new tag.  We add a new tag, but it is not stable.  Does this mean we have to write handwritten code for the new tag-equivalent?  That doesn't seem to mitigate any risk, which is why we need pre-stable tags in the first place.

How could we avoid needing new handwritten?

title: Enable modernize/slicessort rule
body:
That's not really what deprecated means.  It just means that something is disfavored.  We generally do not deprecate something without saying "in favor of ..." so

```
// Deprecated: use slices.Sort() instead.
```

...would be appropriate, IMO
